story:
She was almost completely perfect until the day we came home and saw what she had done. We thought hiring a clone of my wife to help with the baby would be safe—cleaner than an android, gentler, more human. It was my wife’s idea. CLONR promised to filter out “defects of character.” My wife was nervous about that; she’d spent years in treatment as a girl after her mother murdered her father. Our son was four weeks old when we signed the papers. Two weeks later, Rosalee was ready.

Rosalee was polite, pretty—like my wife when we first met, only younger. All clones have blue hair, and their bodies don’t grow fingernails or toenails. She bonded with the baby quickly and doted on both of us. She laid out my clothes, cooked my dinners. But she grew jealous fast. If my wife and I were close, Rosalee would retreat and sulk. CLONR told me it was an adjustment phase. My wife never struggled with jealousy, at least not that I ever saw.

We lived on the fifty-third floor, with a wall of glass over the city. Sometimes Rosalee stood there and stared down at the traffic. “They look like tiny swirling dots,” she would say. “I’m scared of going down there. I’m scared I might get lost.” One afternoon, my wife took our son to a doctor’s appointment—he’d been premature and was growing slowly—and I stayed home. Rosalee turned from the window and said, “Mark, you should have gotten an android.”

She went to her room and came back almost naked, trying to seduce me. When I refused, she slapped me hard enough to cut the inside of my lip. I have the scar. Later, I sold that apartment.

The autopsy said she never experienced sexual contact. That matters. It’s illegal to sleep with a clone, and the police know it. I’m giving my statement again. Please be lenient with my wife. It’s not her fault.

When my wife came home, Rosalee was all smiles, taking the baby from her arms as if nothing at all had happened. My wife asked about my lip. I lied: “I backhanded myself doing exercises.” Rosalee backed me up, cheerful as ever. I felt guilty anyway. Rosalee suggested we go out for dinner. “I’ll take care of the little one,” she said. My wife squeezed my chest. “That sounds wonderful.”

At The Cyberwise Empress, my wife noticed I wasn’t myself. I told her everything. She laughed softly. “She tried to seduce you? Well, she is my clone. The apple doesn’t fall far from the tree. I’ll fix it when we get home.”

It didn’t go that way.

The apartment was dark except for the kitchen. I could smell roasting chicken. My wife went straight to the nursery. Rosalee stood by the window again. “I think your chicken is done,” I said, moving toward the oven.

“Do you remember Vienna, Mark?” she asked. “I fell pregnant then. I knew it would be a boy. I loved his little kicks. He’s not the child I remember.” I told her those weren’t her memories. She looked earnest. “I feel everything,” she said. “I’m sorry. She wants to set you free but will never say it. I can. She never wanted a child. I did. I love you more. Don’t forget—the apple doesn’t fall far from the tree.”

I opened the oven and saw what she had done to my son.

My wife’s scream came from the hallway. She ran in, saw the truth, and shoved Rosalee through the glass. The window shattered. The clone fell.

You’ve heard this from me before. Be kind to my wife for what she endured—but don’t ever let her out.
